Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a strict routine. I'm more of a listener, so I'd put on lecture recordings while doing other things around the house or commuting. I'd maybe dedicate a few hours here and there when I felt like it, but nothing too structured. I found that I learn best when I just absorb information naturally rather than forcing myself into a rigid schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly spend a whole afternoon catching up on recordings. Deadlines would sometimes push me to engage more, but honestly, I didn't stress about it much. I trusted myself to figure it out when the time came, and it usually worked out fine.
Q3: Usually it was to check if there was anything new posted or if a deadline was coming up. Sometimes I'd log in just to download materials so I could listen to them later offline. I wasn't one of those people who checked it every day—maybe a couple times a week at most. If I got a notification about something, that would prompt me too.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd use it when I needed something specific, but it wasn't part of my daily routine. I preferred downloading what I needed and then working offline, so the platform itself felt more like a pickup point than a workspace.
Q5: The audio recordings and video lectures were everything for me. As an auditory learner, being able to listen to explanations multiple times really helped concepts stick. I'd often replay certain sections while doing chores or walking. The downloadable materials were also great because I could access them without needing internet all the time, which matters when you have limited resources.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums and a lot of the reading materials. The forums felt like busywork, and I didn't see how posting there would help me understand the content better. Written resources I'd skim at best—they just didn't engage me the way audio did. I figured my time was better spent on what actually worked for my learning style.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand something I'd be tested on or if it was genuinely interesting. Anything that felt like it was just there to check a box or pad out the course felt like a waste. I'm pretty good at identifying what's essential versus what's filler, and I focused my energy accordingly.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If something wasn't required, I probably wasn't going to do it unless it seemed genuinely useful. Required activities at least had some weight behind them, so I'd put in the effort. But even then, I'd prioritize based on what seemed most valuable for my actual learning and grades.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone without internet. I also recorded myself summarizing concepts and played those back. Since I don't have the most reliable internet access, I learned to work around that by keeping everything I needed saved locally.
Q10: There were many times I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like, I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three or four times while commuting or cleaning, but the system only registered that I downloaded it once. Or I'd discuss concepts with family members to test my understanding. None of that shows up anywhere.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or recording once to download it, then study from that file for days or weeks. The LMS would show minimal activity, but I was actually engaging with that material repeatedly. It's just more practical for me to work offline given my situation with internet access and limited resources.
Q12: There were stretches where I just felt bored with the material, like it wasn't challenging enough or engaging enough to hold my attention. During those times, I'd do the bare minimum to stay on track. It wasn't that I couldn't handle it—I just wasn't feeling stimulated. I'd push through eventually, but motivation was low.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. With limited resources at home, I sometimes had to prioritize other things over logging in. Fatigue played a role too—some days I just didn't have the energy to engage with a platform when I could listen to recordings passively instead. The LMS felt like extra effort I didn't always have.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere. I'd be technically accessing content but not really absorbing anything. Those moments usually happened when I was tired or bored. I'd have to go back later and actually focus, but the initial access was pretty meaningless.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts and some assignments because they felt tedious. I knew they mattered for completion grades, but I just couldn't bring myself to prioritize them over studying for exams. I'm good at tests, so I figured I could afford to slack on the busywork. It was a calculated decision, honestly.
Q16: Deadlines would create brief spikes in my LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as something was due, download what I needed, and then disappear again. They didn't really change how I studied, just when I engaged with the platform. I worked better under a bit of pressure anyway, so last-minute wasn't stressful for me.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much. If I got good marks, I felt validated in my approach. If something needed improvement, I'd note it but not drastically alter what I was doing. I trusted my methods since they were getting results. Feedback was more of a confirmation than a course-correction for me.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I appreciated. I could engage on my own terms without too much hand-holding. But it also meant there wasn't much pushing me to participate beyond what I wanted to do. For someone self-directed like me, that worked fine. I imagine it might be harder for others who need more structure.
Q19: Having downloadable content made engagement easier for me given my internet situation. What made it harder was when things required constant online presence or real-time participation. The gamified task they introduced was kind of interesting but also felt a bit gimmicky—it didn't really change how I approached learning fundamentally.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need, trying to understand the platform, or just browsing without a clear purpose. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. They could also be struggling to understand the material and clicking around hoping something helps. Activity and actual learning are different things.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. I barely used the LMS compared to others, but I did really well on exams because my actual studying happened offline. I listened to recordings repeatedly, thought through concepts on my own, and came prepared when it mattered. The LMS just doesn't capture that kind of engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed something and for how long, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone was actually paying attention or just had something open. They definitely miss offline studying, which for me was the majority of my learning. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually thinking about the material and understanding it, not just going through motions. For me, it's when I'm actively processing information—like really listening to a lecture and connecting ideas. It's not about how many times I log in or how many posts I make. It's internal, mental work.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I listened to the same lecture five times while doing housework, or that I explained concepts to my younger siblings to test my understanding. It wouldn't capture the thinking I did while walking or the mental review before sleep. My engagement was real and deep—it just happened away from any trackable platform.
